executiveSummary:
# Medicaid Program Executive Summary - February 2026

National Medicaid enrollment totaled 74.9 million beneficiaries in February 2026, with all major states experiencing year-over-year declines as the post-pandemic eligibility unwinding continues. California leads enrollment with 12.4 million beneficiaries but posted the steepest decline at -6.8%, followed by Michigan at -7.6% and Illinois at -5.7%. Texas maintains the third-largest program with 4.0 million enrollees and a -3.8% year-over-year decrease, performing better than the decline rates seen in California, Illinois, and Ohio. Only North Carolina achieved enrollment stability with 0.0% growth. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national average per-enrollee cost of $2,883. Texas demonstrates relatively efficient spending at $3,014 per enrollee compared to high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Massachusetts ($4,221), though it exceeds the spending efficiency seen in Florida ($2,036) and North Carolina ($2,623).

Quality performance remains a significant concern, with Texas scoring 62 out of 100 points and ranking 30th nationally in 2024 quality measures. This moderate performance places Texas below leading states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), but above the bottom tier including Mississippi (48) and Oklahoma (51). Texas's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, covering nearly 3.9 million beneficiaries and positioning the state well for coordinated care delivery. Recent federal regulatory activity signals increased scrutiny around provider enrollment moratoria and state directed payments, which may impact Texas's managed care operations and provider network adequacy going forward. The combination of declining enrollment, moderate quality performance, and evolving federal oversight requirements suggests Texas should prioritize quality improvement initiatives while maintaining its cost-effective managed care approach.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with nearly 4 million enrollees, yet demonstrates mixed performance across key metrics. The state's enrollment declined 3.8% year-over-year, closely tracking the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. At $3,014 per enrollee, Texas spending sits modestly above the national average of $2,883 but remains significantly lower than expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Massachusetts ($4,221). Compared to peer large states, Texas falls between cost-efficient Florida ($2,036 per enrollee) and higher-spending Ohio ($3,584), while among Southern non-expansion states, it spends considerably more than North Carolina ($2,623 per enrollee). The state's 93.3% managed care penetration rate substantially exceeds the 83.2% national average, indicating strong commitment to coordinated care delivery models that may help contain costs.

However, quality outcomes present Texas's most significant challenge. With a quality score of 62/100, ranking 30th nationally, Texas performs in the "moderate" tier but lags behind leading states and falls short of optimal care delivery. This places Texas ahead of other Southern non-expansion states like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52), but well below high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). The quality-cost dynamic suggests Texas has achieved moderate spending efficiency but at the expense of care quality—a critical area requiring focused improvement. Key areas to monitor include the impact of ongoing federal regulatory changes around state directed payments and provider enrollment moratoria, which could affect Texas's managed care strategy, as well as potential federal community engagement requirements that could further impact enrollment patterns in this non-expansion state.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 51% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,363 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,883, indicating potential cost control issues or unique population needs.

alerts.title: Florida Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Questions
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,036 is 29% below the national average despite having 3.6 million enrollees, suggesting potential access or adequacy concerns.

alerts.title: Five Southern States Show Critically Low Quality Scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all score below 55 on quality measures, with Mississippi at only 48 compared to the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Combines High Quality with High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts demonstrates the highest quality score (81) while maintaining high per-enrollee spending ($4,221), suggesting effective but expensive care delivery.

alerts.title: Texas Shows Below-Average Quality Despite High MC Penetration
alerts.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (above 83.2% national average) but quality score of 62 is below the national average of 65.6, suggesting managed care effectiveness issues.
alerts.sourceLabel: CMS Quality and Managed Care Data

executiveInsights.title: TX quality score (62/100) lags national average by 3.6 points
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, falling below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the 'moderate' tier, significantly behind top performers like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, contract renewals, and CMS oversight intensity. Poor quality scores can trigger additional regulatory scrutiny and affect HHSC's reputation.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement opportunities to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: CMS announces nationwide temporary enrollment moratorium affecting TX
executiveInsights.summary: Two separate Federal Register entries indicate CMS is implementing temporary moratoria on enrollment for Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP programs nationwide. This regulatory action was announced in May 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment freezes will directly impact Texas's 4M enrollees and $12B budget, potentially affecting contract scope, revenue projections, and operational planning for HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment moratorium details and assess impact on TX contract scope

executiveInsights.title: TX per-enrollee costs ($3,014) exceed national average by $131
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $3,014 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,883, representing a 4.5% premium. Despite this higher spending, Texas achieves below-average quality outcomes.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher costs with lower quality outcomes indicate potential inefficiencies that HHSC and Accenture should address to optimize value-based care delivery and demonstrate ROI.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost-effectiveness analysis needs to HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: New CMS rule on managed care state directed payments published
executiveInsights.summary: CMS issued new regulations on May 22, 2026 regarding Medicaid managed care state directed payments and fee-for-service targeted practitioner payments. This affects how states can structure payment arrangements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: With Texas having 93.3% managed care penetration (above 83.2% national average), payment rule changes could significantly impact HHSC's managed care operations and contract structures.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review new payment rules for TX managed care contract implications

executiveInsights.title: TX enrollment decline (-3.8% YoY) aligns with national unwinding trend
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.8% of enrollees year-over-year, consistent with the national post-PHE unwinding pattern. All top 10 states show enrollment decreases ranging from 0% to -7.6%.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Continued enrollment decline affects contract volumes and revenue streams, requiring adjustment of operational capacity and resource allocation for the Texas contract.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review capacity planning assumptions with TX enrollment projections

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining at 3.8% YoY threatens revenue base and operational scale efficiency.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop retention analytics and member engagement strategies to stabilize enrollment.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating compliance risks and improvement opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Launch targeted quality improvement initiative focusing on top-performing state best practices.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% MC penetration suggests upcoming contract renewals worth $11+ billion in program value.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position for HHSC managed care procurement opportunities through strategic partnerships.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal payment reforms
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS state directed payment regulations create compliance requirements and reimbursement changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish regulatory compliance team to navigate emerging federal payment rule changes.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio and work plan items signal increased audit and compliance scrutiny.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and documentation processes ahead of potential audits.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee spending at $3,014 versus national $2,883 creates margin pressure amid declining enrollment.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement cost optimization analytics to identify high-value care management opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: Nationwide temporary provider enrollment moratoria may disrupt network adequacy and access requirements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop provider network contingency plans and alternative access strategies.

riskOpportunity.label: Digital transformation acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: ESBD procurement portal activity suggests major technology modernization opportunities across Texas agencies.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor ESBD for emerging technology RFPs and position digital health capabilities.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 3,997,752 Medicaid enrollees as of February 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.8% year-over-year, better than some large states.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $3,014 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 managed care enrollees.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement opportunities tracked via HHSC portal and ESBD.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Eleven federal regulatory and OIG items currently active affecting Medicaid programs.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in the current data.
